Prevalência da insulinização de pacientes com diabetes mellitus tipo 2 antes e após a pandemia da COVID-19
trabalhos.resumo: O diabetes mellitus tipo (DM2) é caracterizado pela resistência periférica à insulina e falta de produção pancreática do hormônio. A pandemia da COVID-19 afetou diretamente os pacientes diabéticos, tanto pela ação direta do vírus nas células beta pancreáticas e pela criação de um estado pró-inflamatório que dificultava o controle glicêmico. O objetivo do estudo foi investigar a prevalência da insulinização em pacientes com DM2 antes e após a pandemia da COVID-19. Os dados foram coletados de prontuários médicos em um ambulatório especializado em endocrinologia (CEP 7.453.059). Apesar dos pacientes terem mantido peso e IMC, os principais achados mostram aumento da dose de insulina e do uso de NPH (Neutral Protamine Hagedorn) no período pós-pandemia. O número de pacientes que iniciaram a insulinoterapia nos períodos pré e pós-pandemia foi equivalente. Em conjunto, nossos achados reforçam a piora clínica nos pacientes diabéticos no período pós-pandemia, evidenciado pelo aumento das doses de insulina e manutenção do peso. O estudo contribui para o entendimento da insulinização em pacientes com DM2 em contexto pós-pandêmico, oferecendo subsídios para o planejamento de ações em saúde voltadas à melhoria do controle glicêmico e da organização do cuidado. Nosso estudo é inédito na região e não há relatos na literatura sobre Sars-CoV-2 e uso de insulina por diabéticos

trabalhos.titulo: Acurácia de painéis moleculares em nódulos tireoidianos indeterminados: uma meta-análise
trabalhos.resumo: Background: Thyroid nodules with indeterminate cytology represent a diagnostic challenge and may lead to unnecessary surgeries. Molecular tests have emerged as adjunctive tools for the risk stratification of malignancies. Objective: To evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of molecular tests for indeterminate thyroid nodules. Methods: This systematic review and meta-analysis followed Cochrane Diagnostic Test Accuracy (DTA) and Joanna Briggs Institute methodological guidance and was reported according to PRISMA. Fifty-seven studies comprising 12,303 indeterminate thyroid nodules undergoing molecular testing with histological confirmation were included. Diagnostic performance was assessed across different molecular panels and test versions using pooled sensitivity, specificity, and likelihood ratios. Results: Molecular panels demonstrated high pooled sensitivity (0.91; 95% CI: 0.88–0.93) and moderate specificity (0.57; 95% CI: 0.48–0.66). When restricted to updated molecular panels, pooled sensitivity increased to 0.98 (95% CI: 0.98–0.98), whereas specificity remained limited (0.47; 95% CI: 0.36–0.59). ThyroSeq v3 showed consistently high sensitivity (>90%) and moderate specificity (~50%), whereas Afirma GSC demonstrated similar specificity with slightly lower accuracy. The ThyGenX/ThyraMIR panel showed high pooled estimates but wide confidence intervals. The microRNA-based classifier mir-THYpe demonstrated favorable estimates, although the limited number of studies restricts the robustness of evidence. Conclusion: Molecular testing shows high sensitivity for the evaluation of indeterminate thyroid nodules and may be useful as a rule-out strategy to reduce unnecessary surgeries. However, moderate specificity limits their role in confirming malignancy, and results should be interpreted in conjunction with clinical, cytological, and ultrasonographic findings

trabalhos.titulo: Quem vence a batalha contra a obesidade? Uma meta-análise em rede comparando Tirzepatide e Semaglutide
trabalhos.resumo: Introduction: Pharmacological therapies are recommended for individuals with obesity. Semaglutide, a glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1), and tirzepatide, a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and GLP-1 receptor agonist (GIP/GLP-1), are among the leading pharmacological options for obesity treatment. This network meta-analysis (NMA) aims to evaluate the comparative efficacy of these two agents in reducing body weight and improving glycemic parameters. Methods: Pairwise comparisons within the NMA were conducted using a frequentist approach in RCTs comparing tirzepatide or semaglutide versus placebo, as well as tirzepatide versus semaglutide at their maximum dosages (15 mg and 2.4 mg, respectively). Results: A total of 28 RCTs were included, comprising 34,367 participants, 39.6% of whom were women and with a mean age of 57.8 ± 8.95 years. Tirzepatide demonstrated superiority over semaglutide in percentage weight reduction (Mean Difference [MD] 6.10%; 95% CI: 3.64, 8.57), absolute weight loss (MD 4.55 kg; 95% CI: 1.28, 7.83), BMI reduction (MD 1.71 kg/m²; 95% CI: 0.08, 3.34), and waist circumference reduction (MD 2.89 cm; 95% CI: 1.25, 4.53). Regarding glycemic parameters, tirzepatide was also more effective than semaglutide in reducing HbA1c (MD 0.33%; 95% CI: 0.20, 0.46) and fasting blood glucose (MD 10.39 mg/dL; 95% CI: 4.48, 16.29). Conclusion: In this NMA, tirzepatide appeared to be superior to semaglutide in both body weight reduction and improvement of glycemic indices
